sections.title: 1. German Sentence Structure: Statements vs. Questions
sections.description: In German, the position of the verb is the most important rule.

categories.description: In a regular statement, the verb is always in position 2.

rows: Er kommt aus Berlin. (He comes from Berlin.)

categories.description: To turn a statement into a question that can be answered with "Yes" or "No," you move the verb to position 1.

rows: Kommt er aus Berlin? (Does he come from Berlin?)

sections.title: 2. Answering Questions: Ja, Nein, and Doch
sections.description: German has three ways to answer a closed question, depending on how the question is phrased.

categories.description: If the question is "standard" (no "not" in it):

examples.question: "Sprechen Sie Englisch?"
examples.answer: Ja, ich spreche Englisch. / Nein, ich spreche kein Englisch.

categories.description: If the question contains a negation like nicht (not) or kein (no/none), the answer "Yes" changes to Doch.

rows: Used to contradict a negative question
To agree with the negative statement

examples.question: "Sprechen Sie nicht Englisch?" (Do you not speak English?)
examples.answer: Doch, ich spreche Englisch. (Yes, I do speak it—contradicting the "not".) / Nein, ich spreche kein Englisch. (Correct, I don't speak it.)

sections.title: 3. Polite Requests (Höfliche Bitten)
sections.description: To ask for something politely, Germans use "Konjunktiv II" forms of verbs. This is like the difference between saying "Give me the book" and "Could you please give me the book?"

categories.description: Könnten Sie... (Could you...): Used for ability or possibility. Würden Sie... (Would you...): Used for general requests and actions.

rows: Würden Sie bitte das Fenster öffnen?
Would you please open the window?

categories.note: Note: In these sentences, the main action verb (like helfen or öffnen) always goes to the very end of the sentence in its infinitive form.

sections.title: 4. Question Words vs. Yes/No Questions
sections.description: There are two main types of questions in German:

categories.description: These start with a question word (Wer, Was, Wie, Wo, Woher). The verb stays in position 2.

examples.question: Wie hoch ist Ihre Miete? (How high is your rent?)
Suchen Sie eine Wohnung? (Are you looking for an apartment?)
examples.answer: Answer: Starts with Ja or Nein.

sections.title: Summary Table for Quick Learning
sections.description: Quick reference for all sentence types:

rows: Würden Sie mir den Weg sagen?

sections.title: Pro Tip for Learners
sections.description: When you see a negative word like nicht or kein in a question, your brain should immediately get ready to use Doch if you want to say "Yes!" It is one of the most unique and important words in the German language for daily conversation.
